questions.question: What is the difference between 'filter' and 'backdrop-filter'?
options: filter applies to the element, backdrop-filter applies to the area behind the element
filter is for images, backdrop-filter is for text
filter works in all browsers, backdrop-filter only in Safari
filter is animated, backdrop-filter is static
questions.explanation: filter affects the element and its children. backdrop-filter affects only the area behind the element.

questions.question: Which browser still requires the -webkit- prefix for backdrop-filter?
questions.explanation: Safari requires -webkit-backdrop-filter. All other modern browsers support the unprefixed version.

questions.question: What is a common issue when backdrop-filter is used on root elements like <body>?
options: It doesn't work well because there's no background behind to filter
It causes the browser to crash
It applies to all page content
It's slower than on other elements
questions.explanation: backdrop-filter doesn't work well on root elements because there's no background content behind them to filter.

questions.question: What should you always include alongside backdrop-filter for fallback support?
questions.explanation: Always provide a solid background color as fallback for browsers that don't support backdrop-filter.

questions.question: Which filter function is most commonly used in glassmorphism designs?
questions.explanation: blur() is the foundation of glassmorphism, creating the frosted glass effect by blurring the background behind the element.

questions.question: How do you combine multiple backdrop-filter functions?
questions.explanation: Multiple filter functions are space-separated in one backdrop-filter declaration.

questions.question: What is the main performance concern with backdrop-filter?
options: It requires GPU compositing which is expensive on mobile devices
It increases page load time by downloading filters
It causes memory leaks in long-running pages
questions.explanation: backdrop-filter requires GPU compositing of multiple layers, which can be performance-intensive, especially on mobile devices and large areas.

questions.question: What visual effect do you get with 'backdrop-filter: blur(0px)'?
options: No visible effect — the background is unchanged
questions.explanation: blur(0px) means no blur is applied. There's no visible backdrop-filter effect.

questions.question: Which browser was the FIRST to support backdrop-filter?
questions.explanation: Safari 9 (2015) was the first to support backdrop-filter with the -webkit- prefix, years before other browsers.

questions.question: In which Firefox version was backdrop-filter added without prefix?
questions.explanation: Firefox 103 (2022) was the last major browser to add unprefixed backdrop-filter support.

questions.question: What is the recommended blur range for UI elements like navigation bars?
questions.explanation: A subtle blur of 4-12px is usually sufficient for UI elements. Higher values can reduce readability and increase performance cost.

questions.question: Which CSS feature query can detect backdrop-filter support?
questions.explanation: @supports (backdrop-filter: blur(1px)) checks if the browser supports the backdrop-filter property.

questions.question: What happens to child elements of an element with backdrop-filter?
options: They render normally, unaffected by the filter
They are moved behind the parent
questions.explanation: Unlike filter, backdrop-filter does NOT affect child elements. Children render normally on top of the filtered background.

questions.question: What's the best practice for animating backdrop-filter?
options: Avoid it on large areas — animate opacity or transform instead
Use it freely on any element
Only animate on hover, never on load
questions.explanation: Animating backdrop-filter forces expensive compositing on each frame. Prefer opacity or transform animations for entrance/exit effects.

questions.question: Which is NOT a valid backdrop-filter function?
questions.explanation: shadow() is not a valid filter function. drop-shadow() is the correct function for shadow effects.

questions.question: What CSS property does backdrop-filter NOT work well on?
questions.explanation: backdrop-filter does not work well on pseudo-elements (::before, ::after) in many browsers. Apply it to the actual element instead.

questions.question: What is glassmorphism?
options: A UI style combining semi-transparency, blur, and borders to create a glass-like effect
A CSS framework for building glass UI components
A JavaScript library for frosted glass effects
questions.explanation: Glassmorphism is a design trend that uses backdrop-filter: blur(), semi-transparent backgrounds, and subtle borders to create frosted glass-like UI elements.

questions.question: How does backdrop-filter affect text readability?
options: It doesn't directly affect text, but the blurred background can reduce perceived contrast
It has no visual effect on the overall appearance
questions.explanation: backdrop-filter doesn't apply to children, so text renders normally. However, the filtered background may reduce the perceived contrast of text.

questions.question: What happens if you don't set a background color on an element with backdrop-filter?
options: The blur effect is applied but may not be visible since there's nothing contrasting with it
A default white background is applied
questions.explanation: Without a background, the blur is applied to the area behind the element, but without a semi-transparent overlay, the effect may not be visually apparent.

questions.question: What does 'backdrop-filter: blur(8px) saturate(1.5) brightness(1.2)' do?
options: Blurs the background, increases color vibrancy, and brightens it
Blurs the element itself with multiple effects
Applies blur to the element and saturates its children
Only the last effect (brightness) is applied
questions.explanation: Multiple filters are applied in order: blur softens the background, saturate enhances color intensity, and brightness lightens the result.
